request.question: Smith Creek is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of a river that passes through which US states?

title: Catfish Creek (Iowa)
Cedar Creek (North Fork Shenandoah River)
Rainwater Creek
Passage Creek
North Fork Flathead River
Black Creek (Florida)
Smith Creek (Virginia)
Shenandoah River
Los Baños Creek
Yager Creek
sentences: Catfish Creek is a 21.2 mi tributary of the upper Mississippi River in northeastern Iowa.
It rises southwest of Dubuque, just southeast of Peosta.
The Middle Fork rises west of Dubuque and flows under and then parallel to U.S. Route 20; the former Illinois Central Railroad, now the Chicago Central and Pacific Railroad has its mainline in it.
The remaining forks of the creek are all south of U.S. Route 52.
Swiss Valley Park, maintained by Dubuque County, is along the main fork of the creek, which is a designated trout stream through the park.
In recent years, people have been trying to stop the erosion in this relatively flat way out of the Gorge of the Upper Mississippi.
The North Fork of the creek has been proposed for a trail/park.
Cedar Creek is a 40.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork Shenandoah River in northern Virginia in the United States.
It forms the majority of the boundary between Frederick and Shenandoah counties.
Cedar Creek's confluence with the North Fork Shenandoah is located at Strasburg.
Rainwater Creek is a 3.9 mi tributary stream of Lake Fork Creek.
It flows into Lake Fork Creek downstream from the Lake Fork Reservoir Dam approximately four miles west of Quitman in western Wood County, Texas.
Its primary source is a spring, augmented by stormwater runoff.
It is named for the family of William Walton Rainwater, who was among the earliest permanent settlers of the area.
Passage Creek is a 38.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork Shenandoah River in Fort Valley, Virginia.

For most of its length it flows through a rural valley between the two spine-like ridges of Massanutten Mountain, then exits the valley by cutting a narrow gorge through the northeast end of the mountain.
The North Fork Flathead River (Ktunaxa: "kqaskanmituk" ) is a 153 mi river flowing through British Columbia, Canada, south into the U.S. state of Montana.
It is one of the three primary forks of the Flathead River, the main inflow of Flathead Lake and a tributary of the Columbia River via the Clark Fork River and the Pend Oreille River.
The river is sometimes considered the upper headwaters of the Flathead River, although the North Fork is its official name in the U.S.
Other naming conventions for the river include Flathead River - North Fork, North Fork of Flathead River, and North Fork of the Flathead River.
Black Creek is a 13.0 mi tributary of the St. Johns River.
It is located in Clay County, Florida.
Black Creek has two major tributaries; North Fork Black Creek and South Fork Black Creek.
North Fork originates as an outflow from Kingsley Lake and flows north and then east through Camp Blanding and Jennings State Forest, meeting South Fork in Middleburg, Florida.
North Fork Black Creek has Yellow Water Creek and Big Branch as its main tributaries.
South Fork Black Creek is fed by areas of wetlands and numerous small streams.
The confluence of North Fork Black Creek and South Fork Black Creek near Middleburg form the main channel of Black Creek.
Smith Creek (also known as Smith's Creek) is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of the Shenandoah River in the Shenandoah Valley of Virginia.
Its watershed comprises 106 sqmi within Shenandoah and Rockingham counties on the western slope of the Massanutten Mountain ridge.
Its headwaters lie in Rockingham County just north of Harrisonburg, and its confluence with the North Fork of the Shenandoah River is located just south of Mount Jackson.
The Shenandoah River is a tributary of the Potomac River, 55.6 mi long with two forks approximately 100 mi long each, in the U.S. states of Virginia and West Virginia.

The principal tributary of the Potomac, the river and its tributaries drain the central and lower Shenandoah Valley and the Page Valley in the Appalachians on the west side of the Blue Ridge Mountains, in northwestern Virginia and the Eastern Panhandle of West Virginia.
Los Baños Creek or Los Banos Creek, originally El Arroyo de los Baños, is a tributary stream of the San Joaquin River.
Its source drains the slopes of the Diablo Range within the Central Valley of California, United States.
Los Baños Creek has its source at the confluence of North Fork Los Banos Creek and South Fork Los Banos Creek.
It flows northeast to the west edge of Los Banos and then north to Mud Slough 2.5 miles (4 km) upstream from its confluence with the San Joaquin River.
Yager Creek is a tributary stream of the Van Duzen River on the north coast of California in Humboldt County, California.
It has its source at the confluence of North Fork Yager Creek and Middle Fork Yager Creek.
Its mouth is at the confluence with the Van Duzen River just below the town of Carlotta.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Produce a single, concise, structured first-hop evidence report as the `summary`. The summary must do all of the following and only include content that helps determine what to fetch next:

- Enumerate concrete facts found in the provided passages. For each fact include the passage source label and either a very short quoted fragment (up to about 15 words) when a decisive short fact appears or a brief paraphrase otherwise. Keep each fact to one short line.

- Explicitly identify the bridge entity(ies) and any missing or ambiguous fact(s) that prevent answering the question from the given passages. If a name or identifier in the question corresponds to multiple candidate pages present in `passages`, list every candidate variant and mark the name as ambiguous; do not choose between them.

- Prescribe concrete, prioritized next-hop retrieval targets to resolve each missing or ambiguous element. For each target, specify which page(s) or disambiguation page to fetch and what exact section, keyword, or datum to extract (for example: specific field such as birth year, office held, list membership, dates, or the section heading to check). Prioritize targets (1, 2, 3...) when helpful.

- Only produce a final answer in `summary` if every required fact for every entity is present and unambiguous in the supplied passages; otherwise do not produce a final answer and focus on retrieval guidance.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Keep the summary concise (a short paragraph or a few short bullet-like lines).
- Produce only the single output field `summary` and nothing else.
- Preserve source-linked evidence; do not assert facts without a passage label.
- Do not invent facts or assume which ambiguous candidate is intended.
- Avoid copying long verbatim spans; use short quoted fragments when decisive and paraphrase otherwise.
- When an expected authoritative page is missing from `passages`, note that absence and include that page title as a next-hop retrieval target.

Input: fields `question` and `passages`. Output: one field `summary` meeting the requirements above.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

You are to produce exactly one compact second-hop retrieval query that targets the missing bridge or supporting evidence referenced by summary_1. Follow these operational rules when constructing the single `query` string:

- Use visible anchor tokens from `question` or `summary_1` (named works, titles, artist names, years or other identifiers only if present) and append a short retrieval cue naming the bridge sought (e.g., artist, album, recording, genre, season count, passage, supporting quote).
- Emit one concise line (one string assigned to `query`), roughly ≤ 10–12 words, formatted either as a short interrogative that names the anchors or as a compact keyword string (anchors + bridge keywords).
- If summary_1 explicitly requests a missing attribute or passage, emit the focused retrieval query rather than asking for clarification.
- Do not include answers, conclusions, enumerations, provenance notes, speculative language, or user-facing explanations; do not invent entities or add tokens that are not present in the inputs.
- Include year or other identifier tokens only if they appear in the provided inputs and help disambiguate.
- Keep wording retrieval-oriented and terse. Preserve the fallback that if retrieval using this query later fails, downstream steps may request clarification.

Output contract: produce exactly one field `query` whose value is the single compact retrieval query line described above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions for producing `summary` (single field only):
1. Merge the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into one compact integrated `summary` that preserves the primary answer name(s) and only includes passage-supported facts needed to justify or disambiguate that answer.
2. If `context` already contains a concise answer (a short sentence or a name), keep that content as the first sentence of `summary` when it is consistent with facts in `passages`. If `context` lacks a concise answer, create a concise first sentence that names the primary answer(s), using the canonical labels from `context` when available and otherwise from `passages`.
3. Expand the summary only when `passages` provide explicit, matching second-hop evidence about one or more named entities referenced in `context`. When expansion is appropriate, append at most one short supporting sentence per named entity. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's full name as shown in the inputs and paraphrase up to a few core attributes that are directly present in the `passages` (examples: nationality, a notable award/status, a current affiliation/team, a birth date, membership/partnership relation, or an explicit founding/formation fact).
4. Keep `summary` concise: typically 1–2 sentences for a single entity; allow up to 3 sentences only when multiple distinct named entities each require one supporting sentence. Prioritize the facts most directly necessary to answer the `question`.
5. Only assert facts that are directly supported by the provided `passages` or `context`. Do not introduce new entities, dates, awards, affiliations, roles, or causal claims that are not present in the inputs.
6. Prefer paraphrase over long verbatim copying. Use short quoted phrases only when the exact short phrase (roughly ≤10 words) appears in the `passages` and quoting preserves clarity.
7. If `context` and `passages` conflict, prefer facts that are explicitly present in the `passages`: update the concise answer to match passage-supported content rather than preserving a contradicted `context`.
8. If the retrieved `passages` do not supply explicit supporting evidence for named entities in `context`, keep the `summary` concise and do not expand or speculate.
9. Output exactly one field named `summary` whose value is the integrated post-retrieval summary text. Do not output additional fields, metadata, or commentary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Follow these operational rules when producing `answer`:
- Use only the content of `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2` as your evidential basis. Do not introduce new facts or external information.
- If both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, set `answer` to exactly one lowercase token: yes or no. The `answer` field must contain nothing else (no punctuation, capitalization, quotes, explanation, or additional text).
- Decide which token to emit by aggregating the visible summaries only. Treat `summary_2` as the later integrated evidence; do not let unresolved language in `summary_1` alone prevent emitting the single-token result if both summaries now unambiguously agree.
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, incomplete, hedged such that the relation is unresolved, or if the question is non-binary, do not apply the single-token rule; instead follow the agent's existing final-answer fallback behavior for unresolved or non-binary cases (which may produce a fuller response or an uncertainty signal).

Produce only the `answer` field as specified.

response.hop1_query: Smith Creek is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of a river that passes through which US states?
hop1_docs: Smith Creek (Virginia) | Smith Creek (also known as Smith's Creek) is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of the Shenandoah River in the Shenandoah Valley of Virginia.  Its watershed comprises 106 sqmi within Shenandoah and Rockingham counties on the western slope of the Massanutten Mountain ridge.  Its headwaters lie in Rockingham County just north of Harrisonburg, and its confluence with the North Fork of the Shenandoah River is located just south of Mount Jackson.

Hayfork Creek | Hayfork Creek is a tributary of the South Fork Trinity River in Northern California in the United States.  At over 50 mi long, it is the river's longest tributary and is one of the southernmost streams in the Klamath Basin.  It winds through a generally steep and narrow course north then west through the forested Klamath Mountains, but also passes through the Hayfork and Hyampom Valleys, which comprise the primary agricultural regions of Trinity County.

North Fork Embarras River | The North Fork Embarras River is a 64.0 mi stream which originates in Edgar County, Illinois, and flows through Clark, Crawford and Jasper counties.  Its confluence with the Embarras River is located near the town of Ste. Marie in Jasper County.  It is a major tributary to the Embarras River.  The North Fork's main tributary is Panther Creek, located in Jasper County.

Black Creek (Florida) | Black Creek is a 13.0 mi tributary of the St. Johns River.  It is located in Clay County, Florida.  Black Creek has two major tributaries; North Fork Black Creek and South Fork Black Creek.  North Fork originates as an outflow from Kingsley Lake and flows north and then east through Camp Blanding and Jennings State Forest, meeting South Fork in Middleburg, Florida.  North Fork Black Creek has Yellow Water Creek and Big Branch as its main tributaries.  South Fork Black Creek is fed by areas of wetlands and numerous small streams.  The confluence of North Fork Black Creek and South Fork Black Creek near Middleburg form the main channel of Black Creek.

North Fork Smith River (California) | The North Fork Smith River is 28 mi tributary of the Smith River that begins in the U.S. state of Oregon and ends in the U.S. state of California.  Arising near Chetco Peak in the Klamath Mountains, it flows generally south to meet the Middle Fork Smith River at Gasquet, California.  The combined streams form the Smith River.

Cedar Creek (North Fork Shenandoah River) | Cedar Creek is a 40.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork Shenandoah River in northern Virginia in the United States.  It forms the majority of the boundary between Frederick and Shenandoah counties.  Cedar Creek's confluence with the North Fork Shenandoah is located at Strasburg.

South Fork Crooked River | The South Fork Crooked River is a tributary, 76 mi long, of the Crooked River in the U.S. state of Oregon.  Starting southeast of Hampton in Deschutes County, the South Fork flows east from near Ram Lake (dry).  It passes under U.S. Route 20 at Hampton, then continues east and north for about 6 mi before entering Crook County.  Shortly thereafter, east of Hampton Buttes, it receives Buck Creek from the right and then passes through Logan Reservoir.  Flowing north, it receives Sand Hollow Creek from the left, then Twelvemile Creek from the right before crossing under Oregon Route 380 (Southeast Paulina Highway).  Just north of the highway, the river joins Beaver Creek to form the Crooked River 125 mi from its confluence with the Deschutes River.
response.summary_1:
- Facts from passages:
  - [1] "Smith Creek (also known as Smith's Creek) is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of the Shenandoah River" (quoted short fragment).
  - [1] Paraphrase: Smith Creek is located in the Shenandoah Valley of Virginia; headwaters in Rockingham County and confluence south of Mount Jackson (VA). 
  - [6] Paraphrase: Cedar Creek is a 40.5 mi tributary of the North Fork Shenandoah River in northern Virginia (confirms North Fork is present in Virginia).

- Bridge entity and missing/ambiguous facts:
  - Bridge entity: "North Fork of the Shenandoah River" -> overall river = "Shenandoah River".
  - Missing fact: The supplied passages do NOT state which US state(s) the Shenandoah River passes through (e.g., whether it flows only in Virginia or also in West Virginia). Therefore the question cannot be answered from the provided passages alone.
  - Ambiguity check: "Smith Creek" appears only as Smith Creek (Virginia) in passages — not ambiguous among candidates here.

- Prioritized next-hop retrievals to resolve missing fact(s):
  1. Fetch the "Shenandoah River" main page — extract the "Course" or "Geography" section and the infobox field that lists "Subdivision/States" (exact datum: list of US states the river passes through).
  2. Fetch the "North Fork Shenandoah River" page (if separate) — extract "Course"/"Location" or infobox "States" to confirm the North Fork's state traversal.
  3. (Optional verification) Re-fetch "Smith Creek (Virginia)" full page to confirm no alternate Smith Creek disambiguation; extract infobox/location to confirm it is the Smith Creek referenced.

No final answer is given because the passages do not provide the list of US states for the Shenandoah River.
response.hop2_query: Which US states does the Shenandoah River pass through?
hop2_docs: Tuscarora Trail | The Tuscarora Trail is a 252 mi long bypass route of the Appalachian Trail that passes through the US states of Virginia, West Virginia, Maryland, and Pennsylvania.  In the south, the Tuscarora begins where the Appalachian Trail intersects Skyline Drive in Shenandoah National Park in Virginia.  In the north, it rejoins the Appalachian Trail at the top of Blue Mountain just west of the Susquehanna River and Harrisburg, Pennsylvania, creating a 435 mi circuit known as the Tuscalachian Loop.

Maruia | Maruia is a locality in the West Coast region of New Zealand.  The Shenandoah Highway (State Highway 65) passes through it.  Murchison is 65 km north, the Lewis Pass is 39 km to the south-east, and Reefton is 63 km west by road.  The Maruia River flows past to the west.

U.S. Route 1 in New Jersey | U.S. Route 1 (US 1) is a United States highway which parallels the East Coast of the United States, running from Key West, Florida in the south to Fort Kent, Maine at the Canadian border in the north.  Of the entire length of the route, 66.06 mi of it runs through New Jersey.  It enters the state from Pennsylvania on the Trenton–Morrisville Toll Bridge over the Delaware River in the state capital of Trenton, running through the city on the Trenton Freeway.  From here, US 1 continues northeast as a surface divided highway through suburban areas continuing into Middlesex County and passing through New Brunswick and Edison.  US 1 merges with US 9 in Woodbridge, and the two routes continue through northern New Jersey as US 1/9 to the George Washington Bridge over the Hudson River in Fort Lee.  At this point, the road continues into New York City along with I-95.

Virginia State Route 252 | State Route 252 (SR 252) is a primary state highway in the U.S. state of Virginia.  The state highway runs 29.06 mi from SR 39 near Rockbridge Baths north to U.S. Route 250, US 11 Business, and SR 254 in Staunton.  SR 252 passes through rural areas of the upper Shenandoah Valley of northern Rockbridge County and western Augusta County.

USS Shenandoah (AD-26) | USS "Shenandoah" (AD-26) was one of ten planned destroyer tenders built at the tail end of World War II (orders for four of the ships were cancelled due to the cessation of hostilities).  The lead ship in her class, she was the third United States naval vessel named for the Shenandoah River which runs through Virginia and West Virginia.

U.S. Route 17 in Virginia | U.S. Route 17 (US 17) is a part of the U.S. Highway System that runs from Punta Gorda, Florida to Winchester, Virginia.  In Virginia, the U.S. Highway runs 255.83 mi from the North Carolina state line in Chesapeake north to its northern terminus at US 11, US 50, and US 522 in Winchester.  US 17 is a major highway in the eastern half of Virginia.  The U.S. Highway connects the Albemarle Region of North Carolina with the Hampton Roads metropolitan area.  Within the urban area, US 17 passes through the South Hampton Roads cities of Chesapeake, Portsmouth, and Suffolk and the Virginia Peninsula city of Newport News.  Between Yorktown and Fredericksburg, the U.S. Highway serves as the primary highway of the Middle Peninsula.  At Fredericksburg, US 17 leaves the Atlantic coastal plain; the highway passes through the Piedmont town of Warrenton and crosses the Blue Ridge Mountains on its way to Winchester in the Shenandoah Valley. The route from Tappahannock to Winchester roughly follows the Confederate March during the Civil War to Gettysburg

Tugaloo River | The Tugaloo River (originally Tugalo River) is a 45.9 mi river bordering the U.S. states of Georgia and South Carolina.  It is fed by the Tallulah River and the Chattooga River, which each form an arm of Lake Tugalo, on the edge of Georgia's Tallulah Gorge State Park.  The Tugaloo then flows out of the lake via Tugaloo Dam, passing into Lake Yonah and through Yonah Dam.  The river then ends as an arm of Lake Hartwell, as does South Carolina's Seneca River.  After flowing out of Lake Hartwell, it is called the Savannah River.
response.summary_2: Smith Creek (also known as Smith's Creek) is a 35.5 mi tributary stream of the North Fork of the Shenandoah River. Shenandoah River runs through the US states of Virginia and West Virginia.
